text: Running shoe selection should consider specific shoe category features that differentially affect running economy, performance, and biomechanics.
population: runners with established fitness and training routine

text: Running shoe effects on runners span at least 10 distinct biomechanical and performance domains: running economy, running performance, spatiotemporal parameters, ground reaction forces, joint stiffness, achilles tendon loading, plantar pressure, tibiofemoral load, foot strike pattern, and joint coordination.
population: runners with established fitness and training routine

text: Shoe brand and model specificity matters when evaluating running shoe effects — generic shoe category labels alone are insufficient due to lack of consensus in shoe classification.
population: runners with established fitness and training routine

text: Different running shoe models can meaningfully affect achilles tendon loading and tibiofemoral load, making shoe selection relevant for injury prevention in addition to performance.
population: runners with established fitness and training routine

text: At least 61 distinct shoe models across multiple brands have been studied for their effects on running biomechanics and performance since 2015, indicating rapid evolution in shoe technology.
